description: Bolstering productivity by generating and scaling Design Systems in a click

problem.headline: Problem Statement
problem.body:
While working on freelance project I noticed I was repeatedly doing the same boilerplate for each project and was lacking of a centralized way to actually create, edit and export my design systems for productions.
Many disparate plugins already offer simple basic color scheme generation but they seems very Design Centered and very few actually encompass broader and realistic production scope including:
list: Global hue adjustment to quickly update colors when clients request changes
Fine-tune font scales and color values while preserving visual harmony
Export design tokens in configurable formats (color formats, namespace prefixes, etc.)
Generate configurable color palettes for brand guidelines or handbook documentation

constraints.headline: Constraints
body: Use TypeScript with the Figma Plugin API
Fast design and development (~1 month timeline)
Use modular functions and an Agile approach
Keep architecture flexible for future Figma API changes
Maintain adaptable plugin architecture and data structures

benchmark.headline: Benchmark
benchmark.body: A benchmark was conducted to evaluate existing mechanics and useful interface patterns, such as expandable folder trees and curve-based color fine-tuning tools.

layouts.headline: Dual Layout
layouts.body: Due to the plugin’s complexity, a particular attention was given to accessibility and clear UI guidance. Two layout modes: minified and extended were established early-on to provide either a quick overview or detailed control, adapting to both font and color objects while keeping the interface consistent.

swatch.headline: Swatch Generators
swatch.body: Beyond real-time synchronization, another key focus was style creation. The idea was to provide a dedicated “workbench” where users could craft and automatically generate font sets or color swatches. Since Figma plugins run in a constrained space, custom components were designed to stay flexible and support API interactions without cluttering the interface with too many nested dialogs or unnecessary complexity.

google.headline: Dynamic Shades
google.body: While most external system libraries offer automatic palette generation with minimal options beyond a primary color, the Google Design System library provides extensive configuration. This significantly increased the project’s complexity, although the previously designed workbench sidebar was flexible enough to accommodate these dynamic content changes.

gradient.headline: Gradient Stops
gradient.body: The gradient generator (“Interpolation”) required special design consideration. Unlike typical tools like Adobe Photoshop or Figma, where gradients are created by adding stops with automatic interpolation, a design system requires explicitly defining how many colors are generated between stops. At the same time, the entire interpolation module had to fit within the sidebar, since adding controls outside the workbench would have exceeded the intended interface boundaries.

sync.headline: Synchronization
sync.body: A key challenge was establishing synchronization between the application state and the Figma API, enabling live updates so users can immediately see how their actions impact the design system.

ownership.headline: Ownership Management
ownership.body: Another challenge was defining ownership and responsibilities between the plugin and Figma itself and specifically how different objects behave and interact within the Figma project.

transformer.headline: Set Transformer
transformer.body: A dedicated Transformer acts as a buffer layer that converts complex Figma API objects into simplified plugin domain models while preserving their original IDs for synchronization back to Figma.

library.headline: Libraries Ecosystem
library.body: The Radix UI library is combined with custom widgets to accelerate development while supporting plugin-specific modules. Figma colors ensure native consistency and light/dark mode support, while Radix provides ready-made interaction patterns.

heading: System-level Control
Low Learning Curve
benefits: Aligns with brand and design system needs
costs: Slower first interaction compared to simple palette tools

heading: Complementing Figma
Owning the Source of Truth
benefits: Easier structure decision following Figma styles
Lower risk of conflicts with native features
costs: Must adapt to Figma’s constraints and API design
Requires careful ownership rules (set, map, strict)

heading: Fast Delivery
Scalable Architecture
benefits: Necessary features shipped in time for an upcoming project

heading: Wider Scope
Reduced Feature Depth
benefits: Consistent and centralized workflows across styles
costs: Limits each features deeper development to maintain balance

outcomes.headline: Outcomes & Learning
body: The plugin has received consistently positive feedback and continues to be improved over time.

This project highlighted the importance of using a robust technical stack for complex applications. Strongly typed languages with clearly defined interfaces help maintain consistency and structure, whereas JavaScript’s flexibility can sometimes lead to architectural inconsistencies despite its rapid development advantages.

One key improvement area is the synchronization between the client, the store, and the Figma API. The introduction of asynchronous requests added unexpected complexity, and the overall data flow would benefit from refactoring now that the full lifecycle of the system is better understood.
